events.event: Case opened following a resident-flagged correction: the drinking-water trucking arrangement has been running for a lot longer than a single resolved 2020 episode. Initial sweep confirmed two separate documented trucked-water episodes (2020, 2023), the $8 million treatment-plant fix now under construction, and an open gap on whether the plant has actually been completed as of today. Added E-001 through E-006.

Resident asked directly: what has water carting cost over the recurring years, compared with the one-off cost of the infrastructure fix? Found one concrete carting-cost figure (E-007: $161,000 NSW Government funding for the January 2020 carting operation, alongside an earlier $4 million filtration-system commitment that appears to have grown into the current $8 million project - unresolved which). No cumulative multi-year carting total was found (E-008) - logged as an open gap with a concrete next step (a GIPA request to Council for water-carting expenditure by financial year) rather than estimated or assumed.

Pursued additional investigative paths beyond the initial cost/timeline question: political/governance path (confirmed the Member for Oxley held the state Water Minister portfolio during the exact period of the first 2020 trucking crisis - E-009, recorded as a temporal fact only, no finding of action or inaction); health/regulatory path (searched for a specific documented water-quality guideline exceedance for Willawarrin - found only general characterisations, no specific figures, logged as an open gap - E-010); current-status path (repeated the completion-status search - still no confirmation either way, E-005 gap stands). Applied the Structural Harm Mechanism catalogue against all evidence honestly: 'Time as Structural Harm' is a supportable read of the 2020 commitment-to-2025 construction gap (caveated on the unresolved $4M/$8M link in E-007); an initially proposed second mechanism was checked and retracted as not genuinely supported by the evidence.

Pursued five more genuinely distinct paths after being pushed to actually take 'all paths', not a couple: (1) root cause - confirmed the iron/manganese issue is natural, drought/low-flow driven, distinct from separate historic Hillgrove mining contamination further upstream (E-011); (2) the specific ADWG guideline-exceedance claim was re-tested and could not be independently reproduced on a second search - held at LOW rather than accepted or discarded (E-012); (3) discovered and distinguished a separate $52.9 million Kempsey/Frederickton/Smithtown/Gladstone water project from the Willawarrin-specific plant, preventing the two being conflated (E-013); (4) resolved the previously-open $4M/$8M discrepancy in E-007 - two independent sources confirm it is the same project, which slipped from a 2024 target to 'early 2026' while roughly doubling in cost (E-014, supersedes E-007); (5) found the NSW Audit Office's own 'Local government 2025' report documenting a statewide ~$1 billion rural/regional council water-infrastructure funding gap, placing Willawarrin's situation within an audited systemic pattern rather than an isolated case (E-015).

Pursued the Bellbrook-specific path (this case's title names both towns, but nearly all evidence gathered so far was Willawarrin-specific). Found that Bellbrook and Willawarrin run on separate water schemes (E-016) - meaning the $8 million Willawarrin plant does not, on its own, resolve Bellbrook's water security. No Bellbrook-specific capital project was found anywhere, and no public reporting on Bellbrook's water supply status was found more recent than the June 2020 return-to-supply announcement (E-017). This is a significant, previously-unexamined gap: two towns hit by the same 2020 crisis, but only one has a named, funded, publicly-tracked remedy.

Followed the Bellbrook gap flagged in the previous pass. Found and cross-confirmed (two independent sources) that Bellbrook DOES have its own dedicated water treatment plant, installed in 2009 for $700,000, built after 2005 discovery of Hillgrove-mine-sourced arsenic/heavy metal contamination and a 2008 trucking episode (E-018) - correcting the prior pass's gap. However this raises a new, genuinely unresolved question: the 2009 plant addressed arsenic, not the iron/manganese that caused the 2020 and 2023 crises - it's unclear whether the 2009 plant was simply never designed for that problem, or whether it has since degraded (E-019). Both explanations remain open.

First pass of the ongoing DFAPTI workflow for this case. Six new findings: (1) additional detail on the Willawarrin plant (~130 residents served) alongside an internally inconsistent construction-duration estimate in the project's own public material - '18 months from mid-2025' implies late 2026, not the 'early 2026' target stated elsewhere, logged as a discrepancy rather than resolved (E-020); (2) confirmed the Bellbrook plant's own stated design scope is arsenic/antimony removal specifically, with iron/manganese not mentioned - relevant to E-019 - while also surfacing an unresolved 2009-vs-2010 construction-year discrepancy against E-018 (E-021); (3) found seven years (2016-2023) of independent citizen-science water monitoring at Bellbrook Bridge run with Southern Cross University, including an estimate that the river needs 600-1000 years to naturally leach back to pre-mining contamination levels - i.e. the contamination source is effectively permanent (E-022); (4) found a distinct, earlier (2020) NSW Audit Office performance audit finding the responsible state department had not effectively overseen regional town water infrastructure planning since at least 2014 - separate from E-015's 2025 report (E-023); (5) narrowed (without closing) E-019's open question using E-021's finding (E-024); (6) located the actual public council water-quality annual reports that would resolve E-008/E-010/E-012, establishing a concrete non-GIPA next step, though this sandbox cannot fetch kempsey.nsw.gov.au directly to read them (confirmed via a direct curl returning HTTP 403) (E-025).

Second ongoing-workflow pass. Two prior open/held items were resolved to ACCEPTED on independent re-confirmation: Council's own 'Water supply & quality' page directly confirms Willawarrin, Bellbrook, and the Thunggutti Aboriginal Community are three separate named water supply systems with their own sub-plans, upgrading E-016's inference to a sourced fact (E-026); and the specific ADWG guideline-exceedance claim from E-012 reproduced again on an independent search, clearing the two-confirmation bar for the general claim (though specific guideline figures remain unconfirmed) (E-027). A genuinely new, previously unexamined path was surfaced and logged rather than chased down blind: the Thunggutti Aboriginal Community shares the same upriver supply grouping as Willawarrin/Bellbrook and has not yet been investigated for the same trucked-water history (E-028). The plant-completion gap (E-005) was narrowed with a later data point - still listed as a planned/in-progress project as of the November 2025 Council Meeting, not yet reported complete (E-029). The school-launch detail and cost/GIPA-alternative searches did not turn up anything solid enough to log this pass.

Third ongoing-workflow pass. Followed up the new Thunggutti Aboriginal Community path from the previous pass: Council's own 2020-2021 Drinking Water Management System Annual Report - the exact year of the Willawarrin/Bellbrook crisis - records no customer water-quality enquiries at Thunggutti or Stuarts Point, cross-confirmed across two searches. This is evidence, not proof, that Thunggutti did not share the same crisis that year - it narrows but does not close E-028 (E-030). Also found a dated June 2019 Council media release documenting an earlier shire-wide supply-source switch (Sherwood Borefield to Steuart McIntyre Dam) amid declining river levels - genuine context for the drought conditions behind the eventual crisis, but not itself proof of a separate, distinct 2019 Willawarrin trucking episode beyond the one continuous episode already documented at E-001 (E-031). Plant-completion and ribbon-cutting searches turned up nothing newer than the previous pass's November 2025 data point.
